news_summary: Recent scientific breakthroughs reveal promising advances in treating fatty liver disease, understanding Parkinson's progression, uncovering connections between serotonin and heart valve disease, and discovering how second pregnancies reshape the brain.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées scientifiques récentes révèlent des progrès prometteurs dans le traitement de la stéatose hépatique, la compréhension de l'évolution de la maladie de Parkinson, la mise en lumière des liens entre la sérotonine et les valvulopathies cardiaques, ainsi que la découverte de la manière dont les grossesses successives remodèlent le cerveau.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances científicos revelan progresos prometedores en el tratamiento de la enfermedad del hígado graso, la comprensión de la evolución del párkinson, el descubrimiento de las relaciones entre la serotonina y las valvulopatías cardíacas, y el estudio de cómo los segundos embarazos modifican la estructura del cerebro.

articles.title: Experimental drug reverses severe fatty liver disease by repairing the gut
articles.summary: An experimental drug called DT-109 reversed severe fatty liver disease in animal studies by repairing the gut and preventing harmful toxins from damaging the liver. The discovery could open the door to a new class of treatments for MASH and potentially other diseases tied to gut health.
articles.content:
An experimental drug developed at Michigan Medicine has shown the ability to reverse severe fatty liver disease in animal studies by restoring gut health. The findings, published in The Journal of Clinical Investigation, suggest that targeting the connection between the gut and liver could offer a promising new approach for treating metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH).

MASH is a serious form of fatty liver disease that affects about 7% of people worldwide. It can progress to cirrhosis, liver cancer, and liver failure, yet effective treatment options remain limited.

The investigational compound, known as DT-109, is a glycine-based tripeptide. Researchers found that it reversed MASH in animal models by interrupting a harmful biological process linking the gut and liver.

"We see clear evidence that DT-109 protects the gut epithelial barrier, reducing the systemic influx of harmful microbial products that are thought to contribute to MASH development and progression," said Eugene Chen, M.D., Ph.D., senior author of the study and Frederick G. L. Huetwell Professor of Cardiovascular Medicine at the University of Michigan Medical School.

"This compound shows benefits to the gastrointestinal system and has great potential as a treatment for MASH."

How Gut Bacteria Can Drive Liver Disease

Earlier studies from Chen's laboratory had already shown that DT-109 could improve MASH in animals. The new research explains how the compound produces those benefits.

The team first identified a major contributor to the disease: an overgrowth of the bacterium Clostridium perfringens, which generates ammonia inside the gut.

High ammonia levels damage the lining of the digestive tract, weakening the intestinal barrier. Once that protective barrier is compromised, harmful microbial products can enter the bloodstream, reach the liver, and trigger inflammatory immune responses, including excessive activation of CD8+ T cells.

Through a series of experiments, the researchers found that DT-109 disrupted this chain of events, helping restore the health of both the gut and the liver.

DT-109 Restores the Gut Barrier

In both mice and nonhuman primates, DT-109 reduced Clostridium perfringens levels and lowered ammonia production in the intestines. As a result, the intestinal barrier became stronger, limiting the movement of harmful substances from the gut into the body.

The results were especially encouraging in nonhuman primates, whose liver biology and gut microbiota more closely resemble those of humans. In these animals, DT-109 reduced liver inflammation and significantly improved the severity of MASH.

"DT-109 connects microbiota modulation with liver protection by restoring gut barrier integrity and limiting the systemic translocation of ammonia and other pro-inflammatory microbial products within the gut-liver axis," said Jifeng Zhang, Ph.D., co-author and research professor of cardiovascular medicine at U-M Medical School.

"We also found that DT-109 primarily acts in the gastrointestinal tract, but its reach stretches much further."

Potential Benefits Beyond MASH

The researchers believe DT-109 may have uses beyond treating fatty liver disease.

Previous studies have shown that the compound can reduce the formation of atherosclerosis plaques and prevent vascular calcification in nonhuman primates, suggesting it could also become a treatment for cardiovascular disease.

Because breakdown of the intestinal barrier has also been linked to several digestive disorders, the team believes DT-109 could eventually be explored as a treatment for conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).

Future research will focus on additional testing needed to move DT-109 into clinical trials and evaluate its safety and effectiveness in people.

"This study presents novel evidence about the pathogenesis of MASH and provides excitement about a therapeutic avenue to explore for a condition that remains difficult to treat," said Elliot Tapper, M.D., Academic Director of Hepatology at Michigan Medicine.

"What patients with MASH need is a safe and effective therapy capable of improving their liver and heart health -- of course we are excited about these developments."

Additional authors include Yang Zhao, Ph.D., Ying Zhao M.S., and Yanhong Guo, MD., Ph.D., all of the University of Michigan. Additional co-authors are listed in the published study.

Funding and Disclosures

Ying Zhao, Oren Rom, Jifeng Zhang, and Y. Eugene Chen are inventors on the patent application (Tripeptides and treatment of metabolic, cardiovascular, and inflammatory disorders).

Chen is also an inventor of DT-109. The University of Michigan has patented the compound and licensed it to Diapin Therapeutics. Chen and the university hold an ownership interest in the company. Diapin Therapeutics supplied DT-109 for the study and is continuing to develop the compound.

The study protocol involving humans, all amendments and the informed consent form were reviewed and approved by the Institutional Review Boards at each site, including the First Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University (approval number: XJTU1AF2023LSK330), and the Institutional Review Board of Jinan University (approval number: 2016-017) and the University of Hong Kong/Hospital Authority Hong Kong West Cluster (approval number: UW 20-700). All experimental protocols involving non-human primates were approved by the Laboratory Animal Care Committee of Xi'an Jiaotong University (approval number: 20191278) and the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Spring Biological Technology Development Co., Ltd. (approval number: 201901). The study was performed in accordance with the National Institutes of Health Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where an experimental drug, DT-109, reverses severe fatty liver disease (MASH) in animal models by repairing the gut barrier. This discovery addresses a serious health condition affecting millions worldwide with limited treatment options, offering hope for a new effective therapy. The study provides detailed scientific context, demonstrates broad potential impact on liver and cardiovascular health, and outlines future clinical trial plans, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news with substantial real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Experimental drug DT-109 reverses severe fatty liver disease by repairing the gut
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) is a serious type of fatty liver disease that affects about 7% of people worldwide. It can lead to liver failure, cancer, or cirrhosis, but there are few effective treatments available.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Michigan developed an experimental drug called DT-109, which reversed severe fatty liver disease in animal studies. The drug works by fixing the gut barrier, reducing harmful bacteria and toxins that damage the liver. Studies in mice and nonhuman primates showed DT-109 lowered damaging bacteria levels and ammonia in the gut, which helped improve liver health.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it targets the connection between the gut and liver, offering a new way to treat MASH. Since the drug also showed promise in protecting against heart disease and may help other digestive illnesses, it could lead to treatments for several health problems linked to gut health.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to conduct more tests to ensure DT-109 is safe and effective for humans. These steps will help move the drug into clinical trials, where it can be studied in patients with fatty liver disease and possibly other conditions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The drug DT-109 repairs the gut to reverse severe fatty liver disease in animals, offering hope for new treatments targeting the gut-liver connection.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le médicament expérimental DT-109 inverse la maladie hépatique grasse sévère en réparant l’intestin
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La stéatohépatite métabolique (MASH) est une forme grave de maladie du foie gras qui touche environ 7 % de la population mondiale. Elle peut entraîner une insuffisance hépatique, un cancer du foie ou une cirrhose, mais les options de traitement sont limitées.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université du Michigan ont développé un médicament expérimental appelé DT-109, qui a inversé la maladie hépatique grasse sévère lors d’études sur des animaux. Ce médicament agit en réparant la barrière intestinale, réduisant les bactéries et toxines nocives qui endommagent le foie. Des études chez la souris et les primates non humains ont montré que DT-109 abaissait le niveau des bactéries nuisibles et de l’ammoniaque dans l’intestin, améliorant ainsi la santé du foie.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle cible le lien entre l’intestin et le foie, offrant une nouvelle voie de traitement pour la MASH. Le médicament a aussi montré des effets positifs contre les maladies cardiaques et pourrait aider d’autres troubles digestifs, ouvrant la porte à plusieurs nouvelles thérapies liées à la santé intestinale.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit de réaliser d’autres tests pour vérifier la sécurité et l’efficacité de DT-109 chez l’humain. Ces étapes permettront de lancer des essais cliniques, pour étudier le médicament chez des patients atteints de maladies du foie gras et d’autres affections.

**Une phrase clé** – Le médicament DT-109 répare l’intestin pour inverser la maladie hépatique grasse sévère chez les animaux, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements ciblant le lien intestin-foie.
articles.personality_title_es: El fármaco experimental DT-109 revierte la enfermedad hepática grasa severa reparando el intestino
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La esteatohepatitis metabólica disfuncional (MASH) es una forma grave de enfermedad hepática grasa que afecta a cerca del 7 % de la población mundial. Puede causar insuficiencia hepática, cáncer de hígado o cirrosis, pero hay pocas opciones de tratamiento efectivas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Michigan desarrollaron un fármaco experimental llamado DT-109, que revirtió la enfermedad hepática grasa severa en estudios con animales. El medicamento funciona reparando la barrera intestinal, reduciendo bacterias dañinas y toxinas que afectan el hígado. En estudios con ratones y primates no humanos, DT-109 redujo los niveles de bacterias dañinas y amoníaco en el intestino, mejorando la salud del hígado.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque se enfoca en la conexión entre el intestino y el hígado, ofreciendo una nueva forma de tratar la MASH. Además, el medicamento mostró beneficios en enfermedades cardíacas y podría ayudar en otros trastornos digestivos, lo que podría conducir a tratamientos para varios problemas de salud relacionados con el intestino.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación planea realizar más pruebas para confirmar que DT-109 es seguro y efectivo en humanos. Estos pasos permitirán avanzar hacia ensayos clínicos, donde se estudiará el medicamento en pacientes con enfermedad hepática grasa y otras condiciones.

**Una frase clave** – El fármaco DT-109 repara el intestino para revertir la enfermedad hepática grasa severa en animales, ofreciendo nuevas esperanzas para tratamientos que actúan sobre la conexión intestino-hígado.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed warm-toned painting showing a healthy golden liver gently embraced by a glowing, fortified intestinal barrier depicted as an intricate lattice or mesh, with symbolic blue and green bacterial shapes shrinking away outside the barrier and soft, healing light radiating from the compound DT-109 represented as a small, radiant tripeptide-shaped figure at the center.

articles.title: Yale scientists may have found how Parkinson's disease spreads through the brain
articles.summary: Yale scientists discovered two neuron surface proteins that appear to help spread the toxic protein linked to Parkinson’s disease. Blocking these proteins in mice dramatically reduced disease progression, offering a potential new target for future therapies.
articles.content:
Parkinson's disease may spread through the brain with the help of two proteins found on the surface of motor neurons, according to new research from Yale School of Medicine (YSM). The discovery could open the door to treatments designed to slow or even stop the disease instead of only managing its symptoms.

Parkinson's disease is a progressive neurological disorder in which brain cells gradually become damaged and die. A key feature of the disease is the buildup of a misfolded protein called α-synuclein. As this toxic protein moves from one neuron to another, it contributes to the worsening of symptoms over time.

Until now, scientists have not fully understood how α-synuclein enters healthy neurons after escaping from dying ones. A new study published in Nature Communications points to two membrane proteins, mGluR4 and NPDC1, as critical transporters that help carry the misfolded protein into healthy brain cells.

A New Clue to Parkinson's Disease Progression

Senior author Stephen Strittmatter, MD, PhD, Vincent Coates Professor of Neurology and chair of the Department of Neuroscience at YSM, says the findings could lead to more effective ways to combat Parkinson's disease.

Misfolded α-synuclein is "the pathologic hallmark of Parkinson's disease," he says.

"If we understood how it gets into neurons, we could perhaps block or slow down the progression of the disease," he adds. But to do that, "we need to understand the molecular mechanism of how it spreads."

Tracking How α-Synuclein Enters Brain Cells

Neurodegenerative disorders such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease are becoming an increasingly significant public health challenge in the United States. According to the Parkinson's Foundation, about 1.1 million Americans are living with Parkinson's disease, and nearly 90,000 new cases are diagnosed each year.

The disease commonly causes movement related symptoms including tremors, impaired balance, and slower movement. These problems develop as misfolded α-synuclein accumulates in motor neurons. As the protein spreads to additional neurons, the disease continues to progress.

Researchers suspected that α-synuclein might gain entry into healthy cells by attaching to proteins on the cell surface. To investigate, Strittmatter and his team produced 4,400 groups of cells, each engineered to display a different surface protein. They then tested whether misfolded α-synuclein would bind to any of them.

The vast majority showed no interaction. However, 16 surface proteins did bind to the toxic protein. Among them were mGluR4 and NPDC1, two proteins found on dopamine producing neurons in the substantia nigra, the brain region most heavily affected by Parkinson's disease. The team discovered that these proteins transported misfolded α-synuclein into the cells.

Blocking the Spread of Parkinson's Disease

The researchers next explored whether these proteins were responsible for helping α-synuclein move from neuron to neuron. They genetically engineered mice so that either mGluR4 or NPDC1 no longer functioned, then exposed the animals to misfolded α-synuclein.

Normal mice developed accumulations of the toxic protein in their brains and went on to show Parkinson's like symptoms. In contrast, mice lacking functional mGluR4 or NPDC1 did not. In a separate mouse model of Parkinson's disease, removing the genes for either protein also reduced symptom progression and lowered the risk of death.

Together, the findings indicate that mGluR4 and NPDC1 work as partners to transport misfolded α-synuclein into neurons, at least in mice.

Strittmatter says this mechanism represents a promising target for future therapies. Existing treatments mainly help manage symptoms and do not significantly slow the underlying disease. Blocking the spread of α-synuclein between neurons could provide a way to slow or even halt Parkinson's progression.

Growing Need for Better Parkinson's Treatments

The need for disease slowing therapies is expected to become even greater in the years ahead. Parkinson's disease and other neurodegenerative disorders primarily affect older adults, and the number of Americans over age 65 is projected to rise substantially over the coming decades, increasing the population at risk.

"We have an aging population. How we can stop or slow neurons from dying is an enormous problem," says Strittmatter. "This is really the time to make some inroads into figuring out how to slow it down."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery identifying two neuron surface proteins that facilitate the spread of Parkinson's disease in the brain. This breakthrough offers a promising new target for therapies that could slow or halt disease progression, addressing a major public health challenge affecting millions. The research is detailed and focused, highlighting a clear path toward potential treatments with broad societal impact.
articles.personality_title: Yale scientists identify key proteins that help Parkinson’s disease spread in the brain
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Parkinson’s disease is a disorder that causes brain cells to die over time, leading to problems with movement like tremors and slow motions. A harmful protein called α-synuclein builds up and spreads between brain cells, but scientists did not know exactly how it moves.

**What happened** – Researchers at Yale School of Medicine found two proteins on the surface of brain cells, named mGluR4 and NPDC1, that carry the harmful α-synuclein protein into healthy neurons. They tested this by creating many types of cells with different surface proteins and saw that these two helped the toxic protein enter cells. When they blocked these proteins in mice, the mice showed fewer Parkinson’s symptoms and less brain damage.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it explains a key step in how Parkinson’s disease spreads in the brain. By targeting mGluR4 and NPDC1, future treatments might stop or slow the disease, instead of only helping with symptoms. This is a big step since Parkinson’s affects over a million people in the U.S., and current treatments do not slow down the brain damage.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will likely work on developing drugs that block these two proteins to test if they can prevent or slow Parkinson’s in humans. Further studies will also check if this method works safely and effectively in patients.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Yale researchers discovered two proteins that help Parkinson’s disease spread in the brain, opening a new path to treatments that could slow or stop the disease.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques de Yale identifient deux protéines clés qui aident la maladie de Parkinson à se propager dans le cerveau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie de Parkinson est un trouble qui fait mourir les cellules du cerveau au fil du temps, provoquant des problèmes de mouvement comme des tremblements et des lenteurs. Une protéine toxique appelée α-synucléine s’accumule et se propage entre les cellules du cerveau, mais on ne savait pas exactement comment.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de la Yale School of Medicine ont découvert deux protéines à la surface des cellules cérébrales, nommées mGluR4 et NPDC1, qui transportent la protéine toxique α-synucléine dans les neurones sains. Ils ont testé cela en créant de nombreux types de cellules avec différentes protéines de surface et ont vu que ces deux protéines aidaient la protéine toxique à entrer dans les cellules. En bloquant ces protéines chez des souris, celles-ci ont montré moins de symptômes de Parkinson et moins de dégâts au cerveau.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle explique une étape clé de la propagation de la maladie de Parkinson dans le cerveau. En ciblant mGluR4 et NPDC1, les futurs traitements pourraient arrêter ou ralentir la maladie, au lieu de seulement soulager les symptômes. C’est une avancée majeure car la maladie touche plus d’un million de personnes aux États-Unis, et les traitements actuels ne ralentissent pas les dégâts cérébraux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement développer des médicaments pour bloquer ces deux protéines et tester s’ils peuvent prévenir ou ralentir la maladie de Parkinson chez l’humain. D’autres études vérifieront aussi si cette méthode est sûre et efficace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs de Yale ont découvert deux protéines qui aident la maladie de Parkinson à se propager dans le cerveau, ouvrant une nouvelle voie pour des traitements qui pourraient ralentir ou arrêter la maladie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos de Yale identifican dos proteínas clave que ayudan a la propagación del Parkinson en el cerebro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Parkinson es un trastorno que hace que las células del cerebro mueran con el tiempo, causando problemas de movimiento como temblores y lentitud. Una proteína dañina llamada α-sinucleína se acumula y se mueve entre las células cerebrales, pero no se sabía cómo exactamente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Escuela de Medicina de Yale encontraron dos proteínas en la superficie de las células cerebrales, llamadas mGluR4 y NPDC1, que llevan la proteína dañina α-sinucleína a neuronas sanas. Lo probaron creando muchos tipos de células con diferentes proteínas en la superficie y vieron que estas dos ayudaban a que la proteína tóxica entrara en las células. Cuando bloquearon estas proteínas en ratones, los ratones mostraron menos síntomas de Parkinson y menos daño cerebral.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque explica un paso clave en cómo se propaga la enfermedad de Parkinson en el cerebro. Al atacar mGluR4 y NPDC1, los futuros tratamientos podrían detener o ralentizar la enfermedad, en lugar de solo aliviar los síntomas. Esto es un gran avance porque el Parkinson afecta a más de un millón de personas en EE.UU., y los tratamientos actuales no detienen el daño cerebral.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente desarrollarán medicamentos que bloqueen estas dos proteínas para probar si pueden prevenir o ralentizar el Parkinson en humanos. También harán más estudios para ver si esta forma es segura y efectiva.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Investigadores de Yale descubrieron dos proteínas que ayudan a la propagación del Parkinson en el cerebro, abriendo un nuevo camino para tratamientos que podrían frenar o detener la enfermedad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of interconnected glowing neurons with two distinct, gently illuminated protein shapes—one resembling a lock and the other a key—facilitating the controlled passage of a small, swirling cluster of misfolded protein particles between cells, set against a soft, natural background of subtle earth and pastel colors.

articles.title: Columbia scientists discover surprising link between serotonin and heart valve disease
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered evidence that serotonin, the chemical best known for regulating mood, may also speed the progression of a common heart valve disease in some people. The research suggests that patients with degenerative mitral regurgitation who take SSRI antidepressants and carry a specific genetic variant may develop severe valve damage sooner, potentially requiring surgery at a younger age.
articles.content:
Serotonin is usually associated with mood, sleep, and digestion. But research published in 2023 suggests this chemical messenger may also influence a small structure that performs a vital job during every heartbeat: the mitral valve.

The multicenter investigation found evidence that reduced activity of the serotonin transporter may accelerate damaging changes in valves already affected by degenerative mitral regurgitation (DMR). Researchers at Columbia University's Department of Surgery led the work in collaboration with the Pediatric Heart Valve Center at Children's Hospital of Philadelphia (CHOP), the University of Pennsylvania, and the Valley Hospital Heart Institute.

The study was supported by the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute and was co led by Columbia's Giovanni Ferrari, PhD, and CHOP's Robert J. Levy, MD. The findings were published in Science Translational Medicine in 2023.

A Small Heart Valve With a Critical Role

The mitral valve sits between the heart's left atrium and left ventricle. The left atrium receives oxygen rich blood returning from the lungs, while the left ventricle pumps that blood throughout the body.

Each time the heart contracts, the mitral valve should close firmly. It functions like a one way gate, keeping blood from flowing backward into the upper chamber.

DMR is one of the most common forms of heart valve disease. As the valve tissue degenerates, the thin flaps that should meet neatly can thicken, stretch, or lose their normal shape. The valve can no longer seal completely, allowing blood to leak backward into the left atrium (regurgitation).

This leak can raise pressure toward the lungs while reducing the amount of oxygen rich blood that moves forward through the heart. Some people have no noticeable symptoms at first. As the condition progresses, however, fatigue and shortness of breath can develop.

The heart must also work harder to maintain circulation. Over time, that added strain can permanently damage the heart and contribute to serious conditions such as atrial fibrillation and heart failure. Atrial fibrillation is an irregular heart rhythm, while heart failure means the heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body's needs.

Medicines can ease symptoms and help manage complications, but they cannot reverse the underlying degeneration of the mitral valve.

"Certain medications can ease the symptoms and prevent complications, but they do not treat the mitral valve," says Ferrari, scientific director of the Cardiothoracic Research Program at Columbia. "If the degeneration of the mitral valve becomes severe, surgery to repair or replace the valve is needed."

Current medical guidelines evaluate heart valve disease by considering symptoms, valve anatomy, the severity of the leak, imaging results, and how the heart and lung circulation are responding.

Why Serotonin Matters Beyond Mood

Serotonin is a chemical messenger with jobs throughout the body. It contributes to emotional state, digestion, sleep, memory, and blood clotting. In the brain, it acts as a neurotransmitter involved in regulating mood.

Changes in serotonin signaling have long been associated with conditions such as anxiety and depression. That relationship is complex, however, and neither condition can be explained simply as a shortage of one chemical.

Serotonin communicates by attaching to receptors on a cell's surface. This contact sends a signal that tells the cell how to respond.

A protein called the serotonin transporter (SERT or 5-HTT) helps end that signal. It carries serotonin into the cell so the chemical can be reabsorbed and recycled. This process is known as serotonin reuptake.

Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) reduce the transporter's ability to take serotonin back into cells, leaving more serotonin available for a longer time. This effect can help relieve symptoms of mood disorders.

SSRIs are among the most widely prescribed antidepressants. They include fluoxetine (Prozac) and sertraline (Zoloft).

Because SSRIs reduce SERT activity, the researchers wanted to know whether the same mechanism could unintentionally affect heart valve tissue, particularly in people whose valves were already degenerating.

What Researchers Found in Patients and Mice

The team reviewed clinical information from more than 9,000 patients who had undergone mitral valve repair or replacement for DMR. The researchers also evaluated 100 mitral valve biopsies, which are small tissue samples examined in a laboratory.

"Studying the data of these patients, we found that taking SSRIs was associated with severe mitral regurgitation that needed to be treated with surgery at a younger age than for patients not taking SSRIs," says Ferrari.

That finding showed an association, not cause and effect. The patient data could not prove that SSRIs caused the disease to progress more quickly. Observational studies cannot rule out other differences between groups that might influence when patients require surgery.

The researchers therefore explored the possible biological mechanism in mice and human valve cells.

They studied transgenic mice that lacked the SERT gene and found that the animals developed thicker mitral valves. Normal mice treated with high doses of SSRIs also developed thickened valves. These experiments supported the possibility that unusually low SERT activity can contribute to structural remodeling of the valve.

A Genetic Clue to Greater Valve Vulnerability

The researchers also examined 5-HTTLPR, a region of the SERT gene that helps control how active the serotonin transporter is.

They identified genetic variants that changed SERT activity in mitral valve cells. A "long" variant was associated with lower activity, particularly in people who inherited two copies (one maternal and one paternal).

Patients with DMR who carried the "long-long" variant underwent mitral valve surgery more often than patients with other variants.

Laboratory experiments offered a possible explanation. Mitral valve cells from patients with the "long-long" variant reacted more strongly to serotonin and produced more collagen. Collagen normally gives tissue structure and strength, but excessive collagen can make a valve thicker and stiffer, changing its shape and movement.

Cells with the "long-long" variant were also more sensitive to fluoxetine than cells carrying other variants. The findings suggest that an already damaged valve may be especially vulnerable when serotonin exposure, reduced transporter activity, and genetic susceptibility occur together.

Could a Simple DNA Test Guide Care?

For patients with DMR and the "long-long" variant, the researchers proposed that taking an SSRI could further reduce SERT activity in the mitral valve.

They suggested testing people with DMR for 5-HTTLPR. The test could be performed using DNA collected from a blood sample or mouth swab. In theory, identifying patients with low SERT activity could help doctors determine who might need closer monitoring or earlier surgery.

"Assessing patients with DMR for low SERT activity may help identify patients who may need mitral valve surgery earlier," says Ferrari. "Promptly fixing a mitral valve that is very leaky would protect the heart and could prevent congestive heart failure."

This type of genetic testing has not become a standard part of heart valve care. Major guidelines continue to focus primarily on symptoms, valve structure, leak severity, heart function, and imaging findings. Clinical studies would be needed to establish whether adding genetic testing actually improves treatment decisions and patient outcomes.

What the Findings Do Not Mean

The researchers did not observe harmful effects from normal SSRI doses or the "long-long" variant in cells obtained from healthy human mitral valves.

"A healthy mitral valve can probably stand low SERT activity without deforming," says Ferrari. "It is unlikely that low SERT can cause degeneration of the mitral valve by itself. SSRIs are generally safe for most patients. Once the mitral valve has started to degenerate, it may be more susceptible to serotonin and low SERT."

That distinction is important. The findings do not suggest that SSRIs generally damage healthy heart valves. They also do not justify stopping or changing antidepressant treatment without guidance from a prescribing clinician.

The strongest signal appeared in people whose mitral valves had already begun to degenerate. Even in that group, the human findings were observational and could not establish that antidepressants directly caused earlier disease progression.

The original research raised two practical questions for future studies. One is whether patients with DMR who respond well to SSRIs should receive regular monitoring for signs that the valve is worsening. The other is whether patients who do not respond well to an SSRI might benefit from switching to another type of antidepressant rather than increasing the SSRI dose.

Those approaches have not yet been validated in clinical trials.

Later Studies Expand the Serotonin Connection

Research published since 2023 has added support to serotonin's possible role in heart valve remodeling. Much of that evidence, however, still comes from animals, cells, or relatively small observational studies.

A 2024 study found that mice with deficient SERT activity were more susceptible to fibrotic changes in their cardiac valves and left ventricular heart muscle. Fibrosis is the buildup of stiff, scar like tissue that can interfere with normal movement and function.

That study pointed to HTR2B, one of the cell receptors activated by serotonin, as an important driver of the damaging response. Mitral valve cells appeared particularly responsive to serotonin. The results broadened the concern beyond isolated thickening of the mitral valve, although animal findings cannot determine what happens in people taking standard doses of antidepressants.

Evidence From Another Major Heart Valve

A study published in 2025 investigated serotonin in aortic stenosis, a different type of heart valve disease. The aortic valve controls blood leaving the heart, and aortic stenosis develops when this valve becomes thick, stiff, and narrow.

The researchers compared 38 people with severe aortic stenosis with 38 control participants matched for factors including age, sex, and major medical conditions. Patients with severe aortic stenosis had higher serum levels of serotonin and its primary breakdown product.

The study supported the idea that serotonin signaling may be involved in more than one type of valve disease. However, the research included only 76 people and measured them at a single point in time. It could not determine whether elevated serotonin contributed to the disease, resulted from it, or reflected another biological process.

An Experimental Drug Target Emerges

In February 2026, researchers reported additional evidence connecting low SERT activity with aortic valve disease.

The study examined valve tissue from 66 patients undergoing replacement for severe aortic stenosis and compared it with normal donor valves. Diseased valves showed reduced SERT expression and stronger serotonin receptor signaling.

Researchers then tested the pathway in mice. An experimental compound that blocked HTR2B helped preserve valve structure and improved measurements of blood flow during an early stage of fibrotic remodeling. Human cell experiments also suggested that low SERT activity can make valve cells more sensitive to damaging biological signals.

These results make HTR2B an intriguing possible drug target, but the compound is not an approved treatment for heart valve disease. The mouse model represented early fibrotic changes rather than advanced, heavily calcified aortic stenosis. Additional animal studies and eventual human trials would be necessary before researchers could know whether blocking HTR2B is safe or effective in patients.

A Broader Review Finds an Association

A 2026 systematic review and meta analysis examined clinical studies involving medications that modify SERT activity. The pooled analysis reported a significant association between these drugs and heart valve disease, with an odds ratio of 2.76.

An odds ratio compares the odds of an outcome between groups. It does not directly predict an individual patient's risk, and it does not prove that the medication caused the outcome.

The review also covered a broader category of SERT modifying drugs, not only commonly prescribed SSRIs. Its authors acknowledged that mechanistic evidence remains limited. More detailed research is needed to separate the effects of different drugs, doses, treatment durations, underlying health conditions, and preexisting valve abnormalities.

A Compelling Clue That Still Needs Clinical Proof

Taken together, the findings published since 2023 make serotonin signaling a more plausible contributor to heart valve remodeling. They suggest that the original mitral valve findings may reflect a broader biological pathway rather than an isolated observation.

The research also raises the possibility that doctors could eventually use genetic information to identify vulnerable patients or that scientists could develop treatments aimed at HTR2B. Such a strategy might block harmful fibrotic signaling without broadly interfering with serotonin's many essential functions elsewhere in the body.

Important questions remain. Researchers need studies that follow patients over time, compare individual medications and doses, account for other health risks, and determine whether SERT testing changes care in a meaningful way. Human trials would also be required before any treatment targeting HTR2B could enter routine use.

For now, regular cardiology care remains the practical priority for people with degenerative mitral regurgitation. The evidence offers a compelling explanation for why some damaged valves may deteriorate faster, but it does not replace imaging, clinical evaluation, or individualized decisions about antidepressant treatment.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery linking serotonin signaling to the progression of a common heart valve disease, degenerative mitral regurgitation. This research, involving large patient data and experimental models, suggests potential for genetic testing to identify patients at higher risk and for developing targeted treatments, which could improve patient outcomes and heart disease management on a broad scale.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find link between serotonin and faster heart valve damage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists know serotonin as a chemical that helps control mood, sleep, and digestion. A common heart valve problem called degenerative mitral regurgitation (DMR) causes the heart’s mitral valve to leak, making the heart work harder and sometimes leading to surgery.

**What happened** – Researchers from Columbia University and other centers studied over 9,000 patients with DMR and found that those taking SSRI antidepressants, which affect serotonin, and who had a certain genetic variant, needed valve surgery at a younger age. Experiments with mice and valve cells supported the idea that lower activity of the serotonin transporter protein can make the valve tissue thicker and stiffer.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows serotonin’s role beyond the brain, affecting heart valves. It suggests that a simple DNA test might help doctors find patients whose valves are more likely to get worse faster. This could lead to earlier monitoring or treatment, possibly preventing serious heart problems.

**What’s next step** – Scientists want to do more studies to see if genetic testing can improve care and to test new drugs that block harmful effects of serotonin on valves. Clinical trials are needed to confirm these findings and to develop safe treatments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New research links serotonin and genetics to faster heart valve damage, opening doors for better diagnosis and future treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un lien entre la sérotonine et l’aggravation des maladies des valves cardiaques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La sérotonine est connue comme un produit chimique qui régule l’humeur, le sommeil et la digestion. Une maladie courante des valves cardiaques, la régurgitation mitrale dégénérative (RMD), provoque une fuite de la valve mitrale, ce qui fatigue le cœur et peut nécessiter une opération.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Columbia et d’autres centres ont étudié plus de 9 000 patients atteints de RMD. Ils ont découvert que ceux prenant des antidépresseurs ISRS, qui modifient la sérotonine, et possédant une variante génétique spécifique, avaient besoin d’une chirurgie plus tôt. Des expériences sur des souris et des cellules de valve ont confirmé que l’activité réduite du transporteur de sérotonine peut épaissir et rigidifier la valve.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que la sérotonine agit aussi sur les valves cardiaques. Un simple test ADN pourrait aider les médecins à repérer les patients dont la valve risque de se dégrader plus vite, permettant un suivi ou un traitement plus précoce.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent faire plus d’études pour vérifier si le test génétique améliore les soins et tester de nouveaux médicaments qui bloquent les effets nocifs de la sérotonine sur les valves. Des essais cliniques seront nécessaires.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle recherche relie la sérotonine et la génétique à une aggravation plus rapide des valves cardiaques, ouvrant la voie à un meilleur diagnostic et à de futurs traitements.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren vínculo entre serotonina y daño acelerado en válvulas cardíacas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La serotonina es un químico conocido por controlar el estado de ánimo, el sueño y la digestión. Una enfermedad común de la válvula cardíaca llamada regurgitación mitral degenerativa (RMD) provoca que la válvula mitral filtre sangre, haciendo que el corazón trabaje más y a veces necesite cirugía.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Columbia y otros centros estudiaron a más de 9,000 pacientes con RMD. Encontraron que quienes tomaban antidepresivos ISRS que afectan la serotonina y tenían una variante genética específica necesitaban cirugía de válvula a una edad más joven. Experimentos con ratones y células de válvula apoyaron que menor actividad del transportador de serotonina puede engrosar y endurecer la válvula.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que la serotonina afecta también las válvulas del corazón. Un simple examen de ADN podría ayudar a los médicos a identificar a los pacientes con mayor riesgo de empeoramiento rápido, permitiendo un control o tratamiento más temprano.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren hacer más estudios para ver si las pruebas genéticas mejoran el cuidado y probar nuevos medicamentos que bloqueen los efectos dañinos de la serotonina en las válvulas. Se necesitan ensayos clínicos.

**Frase clave** – Nueva investigación vincula serotonina y genética con daño acelerado en válvulas cardíacas, abriendo camino para mejor diagnóstico y futuros tratamientos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a stylized human heart with a glowing, delicate mitral valve at its center, surrounded by softly flowing serotonin molecules depicted as gentle, luminous orbs weaving through the valve, symbolizing the intricate connection between heart valve health and serotonin signaling.

articles.title: Second pregnancy changes the brain in surprising new ways
articles.summary: Researchers found that every pregnancy rewires the brain in its own way, with a second pregnancy bringing a different pattern of changes than the first. The discoveries could lead to better ways to recognize and treat maternal mental health challenges, including peripartum depression.
articles.content:
A second pregnancy changes the brain in ways that are both familiar and distinct from a first pregnancy, according to new research from Amsterdam UMC published in Nature Communications. Building on earlier work showing that a first pregnancy reshapes the brain, the researchers found that each pregnancy leaves its own unique imprint on the maternal brain.

In an earlier study, Elseline Hoekzema and colleagues became the first to demonstrate that pregnancy changes the structure of the human brain. They also showed that pregnancy affects how the brain functions. For this latest research, the team followed 110 women over time. Some were expecting their first child, some were pregnant with their second, and others remained childless. By performing repeated brain scans, the researchers tracked how the brain changed throughout the study.

"With this, we have shown for the first time that the brain not only changes during the first pregnancy, but also during a second," says Hoekzema, head of the Pregnancy Brain Lab at Amsterdam UMC. "During a first and second pregnancy, the brain changes in both similar and unique ways. Each pregnancy leaves a unique mark on the female brain."

Brain Networks Shift in Different Ways

The researchers found that a first pregnancy produced the largest changes in the structure and activity of the brain's Default Mode Network, a system involved in self reflection, social thinking, and other important mental functions.

During a second pregnancy, this same network changed again, although to a lesser degree. Instead, the most noticeable changes occurred in brain networks responsible for directing attention and responding to sensory information.

"It appears that during a second pregnancy, the brain is more strongly altered in networks involved in reacting to sensory cues and in controlling your attention," explains researcher Milou Straathof, who analyzed the data. "These processes may be beneficial when caring for multiple children."

Brain Changes Linked to Maternal Bonding and Mental Health

The study also uncovered a relationship between pregnancy related brain changes and the emotional bond between mother and child. This connection was stronger after a first pregnancy than after a second.

Researchers also identified links between structural changes in the brain and peripartum depression during both first and second pregnancies. According to the team, this is the first evidence that changes occurring in the brain's cortex during pregnancy are associated with maternal depression.

The timing of these associations differed depending on pregnancy history. Among first time mothers, they were most apparent after childbirth. For women expecting a second child, they were more noticeable during pregnancy.

"This knowledge can help to better understand and recognize mental health problems in mothers. It is important that we understand how the brain adapts to motherhood."

Understanding the Maternal Brain

The findings offer new insight into the remarkable adaptability of the maternal brain. Although most women experience one or more pregnancies during their lifetime, scientists are only beginning to understand how pregnancy influences the brain over the long term.

The researchers say these discoveries help fill an important gap in knowledge about women's biology and could eventually improve care for mothers, including efforts to prevent and treat postpartum depression. The study also highlights the brain's remarkable ability to continually adapt to major life experiences such as pregnancy and motherhood.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about how pregnancy uniquely alters the maternal brain, with implications for better understanding and treating maternal mental health challenges such as peripartum depression. The research has broad relevance for many women and offers potential for improved healthcare outcomes, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substantive detail.
articles.personality_title: Each pregnancy changes a mother’s brain in unique ways, new study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have known that pregnancy changes a woman’s brain, but it was unclear whether a second pregnancy would cause the same changes or different ones.

**What happened** – Researchers at Amsterdam UMC followed 110 women, including first-time mothers, second-time mothers, and women without children. They used brain scans over time to see how pregnancy affected brain structure and activity. They found that a first pregnancy causes big changes in a brain area linked to self-reflection and social thinking. A second pregnancy also changes the brain but in different areas related to attention and sensing the world.

**Impact** – This is the first study to show that every pregnancy leaves a unique mark on the brain. It also found links between brain changes and the emotional connection a mother feels with her child, as well as connections to maternal depression. These findings could help doctors better understand and treat mental health challenges that some mothers face during and after pregnancy.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to use this knowledge to improve mental health care for mothers. Future studies might explore how these brain changes affect mothers in the long term and how treatments can be tailored for women with different pregnancy histories.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Each pregnancy changes a mother’s brain in its own way, helping us understand motherhood and maternal mental health better.
articles.personality_title_fr: Chaque grossesse modifie le cerveau maternel de manière unique, révèle une nouvelle étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques savaient que la grossesse modifie le cerveau d’une femme, mais on ne savait pas si une deuxième grossesse entraînait les mêmes changements ou d’autres différents.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Amsterdam UMC ont suivi 110 femmes, comprenant des premières mères, des mères pour la deuxième fois, et des femmes sans enfants. Ils ont réalisé des scanners cérébraux dans le temps pour observer les effets de la grossesse sur la structure et l’activité du cerveau. Ils ont découvert qu’une première grossesse cause de grands changements dans une zone du cerveau liée à la réflexion sur soi et aux interactions sociales. Une deuxième grossesse modifie aussi le cerveau, mais dans d’autres zones liées à l’attention et à la perception sensorielle.

**Impact** – C’est la première étude à montrer que chaque grossesse laisse une empreinte unique sur le cerveau. Elle a aussi trouvé des liens entre ces changements cérébraux et le lien émotionnel entre la mère et l’enfant, ainsi que des connexions avec la dépression maternelle. Ces résultats pourraient aider les médecins à mieux comprendre et traiter les troubles mentaux que certaines mères rencontrent pendant et après la grossesse.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent utiliser ces connaissances pour améliorer les soins en santé mentale pour les mères. Les futures études pourraient explorer comment ces changements cérébraux affectent les mères à long terme et comment adapter les traitements selon l’histoire de grossesse.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Chaque grossesse modifie le cerveau maternel à sa manière, ce qui aide à mieux comprendre la maternité et la santé mentale des mères.
articles.personality_title_es: Cada embarazo cambia el cerebro de la madre de forma única, revela nuevo estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos sabían que el embarazo cambia el cerebro de una mujer, pero no estaba claro si un segundo embarazo causaría los mismos cambios o diferentes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Amsterdam UMC siguieron a 110 mujeres, incluyendo madres primerizas, madres en su segundo embarazo y mujeres sin hijos. Usaron escáneres cerebrales para observar cómo el embarazo afectaba la estructura y la actividad del cerebro. Encontraron que el primer embarazo produce grandes cambios en una parte del cerebro relacionada con la reflexión sobre uno mismo y el pensamiento social. El segundo embarazo también cambia el cerebro, pero en áreas diferentes relacionadas con la atención y la percepción sensorial.

**Impacto** – Este es el primer estudio que muestra que cada embarazo deja una marca única en el cerebro. También encontró vínculos entre los cambios cerebrales y el vínculo emocional entre madre e hijo, así como conexiones con la depresión materna. Estos hallazgos podrían ayudar a los médicos a entender mejor y tratar los problemas de salud mental que enfrentan algunas madres durante y después del embarazo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan usar este conocimiento para mejorar la atención de salud mental para las madres. Estudios futuros podrían explorar cómo estos cambios cerebrales afectan a las madres a largo plazo y cómo adaptar los tratamientos según el historial de embarazo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Cada embarazo cambia el cerebro de la madre de manera única, ayudando a comprender mejor la maternidad y la salud mental materna.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two overlapping, stylized female brain silhouettes composed of delicate, intertwining neural networks glowing softly, with one brain subtly larger and textured to represent the first pregnancy’s deep changes in self-reflection and social thinking areas, while the second brain highlights intricate, vibrant pathways symbolizing attention and sensory response networks, all set against a gentle, natural-toned background evoking growth and maternal connection.
